passText: You noticed, didn't you. Everyone else sees someone angry and fighting back, but you saw the part underneath. The truth is I stopped sleeping the week it started, not because I was afraid of the accusation, but because I realized how alone I was in it. Years of neighbors and not one of them came to my door to ask if it was true before deciding it was.
failText: I appreciate the concern, but I'm handling it. Some things you just work through on your own.

passText: You can see it on me, can't you. I keep saying I'm angry on their behalf, but the truth is I'm terrified. My apprentice offered to confess to it just to make the pressure stop, and I had to talk them out of it. That's what false accusations do to a young person who did nothing wrong.
failText: I'm fine. It's them I'm worried about, not me.

passText: You caught the part I was hoping nobody would question. I said people started talking, like it happened on its own, but it didn't. I was the first voice, the one who saw the patient getting worse and said it out loud, and the rest of the settlement followed my lead because I sounded certain.
failText: I made assumptions I shouldn't have. The details don't change what needs to happen now.

passText: You're right, those two things don't fit together. I said it slipped out, but you can hear that the words were too precise for an accident. I chose them because I knew exactly where she was vulnerable, and calling it a slip is the story I've been telling myself so I can sleep.
failText: It's complicated. Family things usually are.

passText: You picked up on something I left out, didn't you. I called it an argument, like we were both fighting, but I wasn't angry. I was calm when I said it and the words were chosen, not thrown. That's the part that doesn't fit the version I've been telling, because there was no heat and I just wanted to win.
failText: I said something I regret. That's where I'd like to leave it.

passText: You've put your finger on the thing that doesn't add up. I said I was trying to help, but my cousin never asked me to speak and the council never invited outside voices, so I pushed my way in. When you line those facts up it stops looking like concern and starts looking like what it was. I wanted to feel useful more than I wanted to help.
failText: I was only trying to help. That's really all there is to say about it.

passText: You've found the gap in my story, haven't you. I said I took over when they were struggling, but they started recovering months before I let go, and the timeline doesn't hold up if my motive was really concern. The truth is I enjoyed being in charge because it made me feel competent and needed. My sibling's crisis gave me a role I didn't want to give back.
failText: I overstepped and I'm correcting it. That's the whole of it.

passText: You can see it, can't you. I keep calling it an honest mistake, but something in my face is telling you otherwise. The truth is I was careless because part of me didn't want my friend's attention going to someone new, and we've been close for years. When I heard a rumor about someone with a similar name I didn't bother checking because I wanted it to be true.
failText: I mixed up two people and spoke too soon. That's really all there is to it.

passText: You know about families bending the old rules? I stopped bringing it up because nobody here remembers a time when it actually happened, and hearing you say it changes something. The truth is I almost surrendered last month when my family introduced me to someone suitable, and I nearly said yes just to end the fight. I was ready to give up everything I wanted to avoid a battle nobody remembers anyone winning.
failText: It's a tradition that goes back a long way. I don't think the history changes what I'm dealing with now.
